Charles Martindale, "Green Politics: the Eclogues ," CCV
107-124.
Alpers' fine study of pastoral includes Theocritus, Vergil and Shakespeare and stresses modern incarnations. Martindale explores the
relationship between the political and the aesthetic within the Eclogues
and within writing about them. He speculates at the close: "Whether the
current preoccupation with the environment and the greening of
politics will turn poets back to the Eclogues remains to be seen (122)."

Paul Murgatroyd, "Aristaeus and Mount Lycae
140 (1997) 285-290.
Kraggerud provides a meticulous commentary on the proem to
Georgics 3, 1-48 and argues that the poem honoring Caesar (Octavian)
was an Aeneid closely resembling the extant epic. The proem does not
support the idea that Vergil altered his design for a contemporary epic
on Caesar to a mythical-historical epic on Aeneas. Murgatroyd accents
parallels in Aristaeus' earlier trip to the source of the Peneus (534ff)
and Orpheus' visit to Taenarum and Hades (467 ff). Orpheus' rìte de
passage requiring bravery and devotion is more terrifying and futile.

raw_text:
Richard F. Thomas, "The isolation of Turnus ( Aeneid ,
Book 12)," AEPC 271-302.
Hughes assesses Creusa' s importance for the epic and her tragic loss;
she illuminates Aeneas' character as a vital, valued part of his life. Laird
examines characterisation as relation between poet and character,
highlighting Vergil's distinctive techniques of psychological characterisation (283-287), and as relation between text and audience (287-292),
thereby underscoring the constitutive role of readers and audience in
determining characterisation. Setaioli' s study of Palinurus extends to
Dante and Ungharetti. Thomas contests assertive Augustan readings of
Turnus which see him from the perspective of Aeneas and in relation to
his obstruction to Aeneas' mission. To focus on Turnus' rage and
anger is, for Thomas, "a huge simplification of the complexity of
Vergil (285)." Thomas' counter-armament is formidable: the aristeia,
devotio, his armor, behavior before and after Allecto, the similes,
prognostication, and sensitivity to his isolation with an inimical Jupiter.

raw_text:
James Zetzel, "Rome and its traditions," CCV 188-203.
Luck-Heyse studies the depiction of flight in Homer, Vergil, and Ovid.
Wifstrand-Schiebe examines the story of Saturn, Vergil's picture of
Saturn's reign in Latium, and the genealogy of Latinus. Zetzel notices
that Vergil's account of Rome is marked by a combination of change
with permanence, and of surface clarity with discordant details.
Prophecies , which feature in Aeneid 1, 6, and 8, share similar content
but show marked differences too; "there is very little overlap in specific
content; the focus of each is different; and each is affected by its
context within the poem as a whole (196)." Finally, "Rome's past, and
its future, are what the reader will make of them (202)."

R. J. Tarrant, "Poetry and Power: Virgil's poetry in
contemporary context," CCV 169-187.
Gottlieb associates key passages in Aeneid 1, 8, and 12 with Augustan
religious policy highlighting the use of symbols in politics, political
propaganda and its function, political community and cult community.
Stahl' s introduction to AEPC offers an excursus on the role of
linguistic details, a survey of changing trends in Aeneid interpretation
accenting the wide variety of methodological approaches to the e
and to its political contexts. Tarrant accepts that Vergil's poe
engages seriously with political realities of his time while distan
itself from those realities, a stance which has been identified as
ambivalent or multivocal. But the ambivalent or pessimistic outlook has
bearing particularly on the poet's conception of human nature.
Tarrant's explanation for the chiaroscuro "reading" of the epic is
arresting: "the ambivalence has become more prominent in recent
Virgilian criticism at the same time as ancient historians have begun to
replace Syme's dark image of the princeps with more nuanced
interpretations (186)."

199, Charles Marie Ternes, "Securos latices et longa obliuia
(Aen. 6. 703-723). On Comprehension levels in the Aeneid and
Ausonius' Moseila ," BAL 26 (1997) 124-153.
Markus deals with Statius' recreation of Vergil's Nisus and Euryalus in
his Hopleus and Dymas episode in Thebaid 10, 347-448. Both poets
engage with popular paradigms of heroism and self-inflicted death,
suggesting reflection on epic's function as a public medium. Tarrant
treats shifting literary tastes of the mid and late first century AD, the
Flavian period, and the Christian era. The "conjunction of his poetry
with imperial ideology... is a recurring feature of his ancient reception
(71)." Temes' paper explores Vergilian influence on Ausonius, trends
in scholarship relating to the contexts of Vergil's epic, divine action
and heroism and their Augustan implications. Analysis of Aeneid 6,
703-723 precedes assessment of Ausonius' Mosella, dedicated to
Nature and the countryside. The two poets are markedly different, but
both share "The quest for a peaceful abundance and the calming down
of ancient terrors ...Excludit veteres nova gratia curas [Mos. 207]."

90.
Burrow traces Vergil reception from Dante, "the most sophisticated renovator of Virgil (80)," through Ariosto, Tasso, and
Spenser. For the Renaissance Vergil provided "the concept of an
encomiastic epic, which praises a nation state and its ruler and which
seeks to manifest the strengths of a vernacular tongue (86)." Milton's
Vergil was shorn of his one-time role as moral guide; he had become
"a model of how to insinuate one's poem into the complex of
intertextual relations within which epic poems make their significance
(90)."

Cox's 'envoi' focuses on Hermann Broch's novel as forerunner of
Adam Parry's perception, "The two voices of Virgil's Aeneid " (1963),
the public voice of triumph and the private voice of regret. "To say
that The Death of Virgil is the twentieth century's most important
response to Virgil seems no exaggeration (335)." Kennedy examines
the implications of T. S. Eliot's engagement with Vergil and the
ambivalence of his use of the word "tradition," and particularly the
way in which Vergil's epic functions as a usable past for Eliot.
Kennedy's interpretation configures the epic as a dramatic allegory of
the act of historical understanding with an exploration of some of the
issues involved. The Aeneid offers its readers, whatever their preoccupations, who search for the reassurance of shape and closure, an end,
"a memorably elaborated and seductively attractive response: a sense
of destiny, an end to history in impérium and the role-complex of
authority and submission it offers (55)."
